summary: My name is Wataru Ishisaka, born in 1906 in Kumamoto Prefecture. I came to the United States in 1923 when I was 18 years old, following my father's footsteps. I had a strong yearning for America, and although I was ready to attend Waseda University in Japan, my father called me to the States. Growing up, I was quite a fighter and had many memorable experiences both in Japan and the US.

I was born in Kumamoto Prefecture in 1906, a place where my family had deep roots. My great-grandfather's family settled there after moving from Iwami Province, and our family graves are located there with large round stones. Kumamoto was my childhood home before I moved to the United States. It holds many memories, including visits to Suisenji Temple and the influence of the Hosokawa clan.

My father fought in the Russo-Japanese War around the time I was born in 1906. He described it as a very cruel and fierce battle, especially compared to the Sino-Japanese War. He witnessed horrific scenes at places like Horten and Diren, where soldiers suffered terribly in harsh conditions. His experiences made him strongly opposed to war and influenced his decision to avoid sending his sons to fight.

The United States became my family's new home starting in 1908 when my father first came here. I followed in 1923 at age 18, and my father stayed until 1951. Life here was different from Japan; my mother even preferred American customs, like the style of casket for burial. Despite challenges, the US offered us opportunities and a way to avoid the wars in Japan. My mother eventually joined us here in 1953 after some difficulties obtaining a visa.

My uncle lived in Tokyo but went bankrupt and mortgaged all his property before coming to the United States. Unfortunately, he never returned to Japan, which was a source of sadness for our family. Tokyo was where some of my family’s troubles began, influencing my father’s decision to come to America. It was a place tied to both hardship and family separation.

My father lived in Walnut Grove, California, after coming to the United States in 1908. He was there when I was young, and he returned briefly to Japan for his mother's funeral but had to borrow money from my mother. Walnut Grove was part of his life in America, where he stayed for many years until 1951. It was a place connected to his efforts to avoid war and build a new life.

Before coming to the United States, my family stayed in Nagasaki for about twenty days. It was part of our journey from Japan to America, and a significant moment as we prepared for a new life abroad. Nagasaki was a brief but important stop on the way to the US, marking the transition from our old home to the new.

My great-grandfather’s family originally came from Iwami Province, where our family lineage goes back about 1,100 years. After losing battles, his tribe finally settled in Kumamoto. This history was important to our family identity and pride. The family graves in Kumamoto reflect this long heritage from Iwami.

Kumamoto was my birthplace and where my family settled after moving from Iwami Province. It was home to our family graves and the place where I spent my childhood. I have fond memories of visiting Suisenji Temple there, which was created by the Hosokawa clan. Kumamoto represents my roots and early life before moving to the United States.

When my mother was initially refused a visa to come to the United States after my father's death, I sought help from the Mayor of Sacramento. He agreed it was unreasonable and wrote letters to the Consulate General of the United States in Fukuoka. Thanks to his intervention, my mother was able to join us in America in 1953. Sacramento played a key role in reuniting our family.

entity: Consulate General of the United States, Fukuoka
summary: The Consulate General of the United States in Fukuoka initially refused my mother's visa applications five times after my father died. It was only after the Mayor of Sacramento intervened by sending letters that the consulate approved her visa. This organization was a significant hurdle in our family's reunion but eventually allowed my mother to come to the US in 1953.

As a child around four or five years old, I visited Suisenji Temple, which was created by Lord Hosokawa. It was a pleasant memory from my early years in Kumamoto, and I have never forgotten it. The temple connects to the history of the Hosokawa clan and represents a peaceful part of my childhood before moving abroad.

The Hosokawa clan was historically important in Kumamoto, where Suisenji Temple was created by one of their lords. This clan's legacy was part of the cultural and historical backdrop of my childhood. Their influence is still remembered in the places I visited growing up in Kumamoto.

I was ready to attend Waseda University in Japan, which was founded by Ōkuma Shigenobu, a member of the Liberal Party, around the 20th year of the Meiji era. However, my father called me to come to the United States instead. Waseda represented the educational path I initially planned before choosing to pursue life in America.

My older brother was preparing to attend Meiji University in Japan before our father called us to come to the United States. Like me with Waseda University, he had plans for higher education in Japan. Meiji University was part of the educational opportunities we considered before moving abroad.

Ōkuma Shigenobu was a politician who founded Waseda University during the 20th year of the Meiji era. He was a member of the Liberal Party in Japan. His role in establishing Waseda is part of the educational history that influenced my plans before I came to the United States.

The Liberal Party, active around 1890 in Japan, included politicians like Ōkuma Shigenobu who founded Waseda University. This party was part of the political context during the Meiji era that shaped educational reforms. It indirectly influenced my educational opportunities in Japan before I moved to America.

My father fought in the Sino-Japanese War and said it was not as bad as the Russo-Japanese War. He often contrasted the two wars, describing the Russo-Japanese War as much more brutal. His experiences in these wars shaped his strong opposition to war and his desire to keep his sons from fighting.

During the Russo-Japanese War, my father was involved in battles around Horten, where he witnessed savage fighting and many casualties. He rode on horseback carrying water to soldiers who were begging for it, both Japanese and Russian. The brutal scenes at Horten deeply affected him and reinforced his anti-war stance.

My father's troop chased Russian forces to Diren during the Russo-Japanese War. This was part of the fierce battles he endured, which left him physically diminished and emotionally scarred. Diren was one of the harsh locations where he experienced the brutal realities of war.
